%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.12 Summary

> **Section Objective:** Review the Chapter 1 ideas needed to recognize, measure, and classify signals and systems.

---

**Concepts In This Section**

- signals
- systems
- energy
- power
- continuous-time signals
- discrete-time signals
- analog signals
- digital signals
- periodic signals
- fundamental period

## 1. Signals and Systems

A **signal** is data or information that carries meaning — it can be a voltage waveform, an audio recording, or a temperature sequence. A **system** is anything that receives an input signal and produces an output signal or response. Systems can be hardware (a filter circuit) or software (an algorithm).

**Representative example:** A microphone captures a noisy audio signal. A noise-reduction algorithm — the system — processes that input and outputs a cleaner audio signal. The algorithm is the system; the waveforms are the signals.

### EXAM TIP

If a problem uses the words **input**, **output**, **response**, or **processes a signal**, the system viewpoint is being tested. Identify what is doing the processing — that is the system.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="web_reference_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![File:Superposition principle block diagram for a MIMO system.png](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/be/Superposition_principle_block_diagram_for_a_MIMO_system.png?utm_source=commons.wikimedia.org&utm_campaign=imageinfo&utm_content=original)
*An input signal enters the system block and leaves as an output response — the system is the processing step in between.*
%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-reference-source"><a href="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/be/Superposition_principle_block_diagram_for_a_MIMO_system.png?utm_source=commons.wikimedia.org&utm_campaign=imageinfo&utm_content=original" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Reference image from Wikimedia Commons</a></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Signal Energy

This is the **continuous-time signal energy formula**.

- \(x(t)\) is the signal value at time \(t\).
- \(|x(t)|^2\) is the squared magnitude — always non-negative.
- The integral accumulates that squared size over **all time**, from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\).

**When to use it:** When a question asks for total signal size over time, or asks whether a signal has finite energy.

**Representative example:** A rectangular pulse that is nonzero only on a finite interval typically has finite energy because the integral has a bounded contribution.

#### Common Misuse

Do **not** average when the question asks for energy. Dividing by a time window belongs to the power formula, not the energy formula.

$$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |x(t)|^2\,dt$$

## 3. Time Axis vs Amplitude Axis

**Continuing the energy-versus-power concept:**

This is the **continuous-time average power formula**. Power is time-averaged energy: the integral accumulates squared magnitude over a symmetric window \([-T, T]\), then divides by the window length \(2T\), and finally takes the limit as \(T \to \infty\).

**When to use it:** When total energy is infinite but the signal has a stable average squared size — a signal that never dies out.

**Representative example:** A nonzero sinusoid that lasts forever has infinite total energy, but its average power settles to a finite positive constant (half the squared amplitude).

#### Common Misuse

Do **not** call every infinite-energy signal a power signal. The limit must exist and be **finite**. If average power is also infinite, the signal is neither an energy signal nor a power signal.

$$P_x = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}|x(t)|^2\,dt$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 4. Periodic Signals and Fundamental Period

Two independent classification axes describe any signal:

- **Horizontal (time) axis:** Is the signal defined at every instant, or only at isolated sample points?
  - **Continuous-time** — defined for every \(t\) in an interval.
  - **Discrete-time** — defined only at integer or equally spaced sample indices.
- **Vertical (amplitude) axis:** Can the signal take any value, or only values from a finite set?
  - **Analog** — amplitude can be any real value.
  - **Digital** — amplitude is restricted to a finite set of quantized levels.

### CLASSIFICATION TABLE

| | **Analog amplitude** | **Digital amplitude** |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous-time** | Continuous-time analog | Continuous-time digital |
| **Discrete-time** | Discrete-time analog | Discrete-time digital |

**Representative example:** A temperature sensor that reports one rounded integer value every minute produces a **discrete-time digital** signal — discrete because samples occur only at minute marks, digital because values are rounded to a finite set.

### EXAM TIP

Do **not** confuse discrete-time with digital. Discrete-time is about **when** samples exist (time axis). Digital is about **what values** are allowed (amplitude axis). They are independent properties.

## 4. Periodic Signals and Fundamental Period

This equation is the **periodicity condition**.

- \(T_0 > 0\) is a positive time shift that leaves the **entire** signal unchanged.
- The equation must hold for **all** \(t\), not just at a few points.
- The **fundamental period** is the smallest positive \(T_0\) for which this holds.

**When to use it:** When a problem asks whether a signal repeats exactly, or asks you to find the period.

**Representative example:** If a waveform repeats every 2 seconds and no smaller positive shift reproduces the full signal, then \(T_0 = 2\) seconds is the fundamental period.

#### Common Misuse

A signal that has a repeated-looking segment over a few cycles is **not** periodic unless the repetition holds over all time from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\). A finite burst that stops is never periodic.

$$x(t) = x(t + T_0)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- A **signal** carries data or information. A **system** processes an input signal to produce an output response — it may be hardware or software.
- **Signal energy** (total squared size over all time): $$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |x(t)|^2\,dt$$
- **Average power** (time-averaged squared size): $$P_x = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}|x(t)|^2\,dt$$
- A finite-duration pulse typically has **finite energy** and zero average power. A nonzero sinusoid lasting forever typically has **infinite energy** but finite average power.
- **Time axis** classification: continuous-time (defined for all \(t\)) vs. discrete-time (defined only at sample points).
- **Amplitude axis** classification: analog (any real value) vs. digital (finite quantized set). These two axes are independent — do not confuse discrete-time with digital.
- **Periodicity condition**: $$x(t) = x(t + T_0)$$  The smallest positive \(T_0\) satisfying this for all \(t\) is the **fundamental period**.
- A finite repeated-looking burst is **not** periodic — true periodicity requires exact repetition over all time from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\).

*Next, use these ideas as the vocabulary and formula triggers for solving signal and system problems.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
